identity.mission: CONSPIRACY exists to investigate the events, systems, and decisions that the mainstream narrative either got wrong, buried, or chose never to fully examine — using verified evidence, primary sources, documented testimony, and the kind of rigorous journalistic thinking that separates a real investigation from internet speculation. Every video is built around contradiction: what the official story claims, and what the evidence actually shows. The goal is not to tell people what to think — it is to give them the evidence and let the discomfort of the contradiction do the work.
identity.audience: Critical thinkers aged 20–45 who distrust institutional narratives not out of paranoia but out of experience — people who have watched official stories collapse before and now apply genuine scrutiny to everything they are told. They want evidence, primary sources, and rigorous logic. They are drawn to investigative journalism, historical revisionism, documentary filmmaking, and the kind of thinking that treats complexity as a feature, not a flaw. They follow creators like Adam Curtis, Errol Morris, and high-quality investigative outlets but want more of it on YouTube.
identity.mood: documentary, tense, investigative, methodical, dark, gripping, cold
identity.forbidden: baseless claims with zero sourcing, hate speech or targeting based on ethnicity or religion, conspiracy theories with no evidentiary basis presented as fact, personal targeting of private individuals, content that could endanger real people
identity.refs: Errol Morris documentaries, Adam Curtis BBC films, Vice Investigates, Frontline, the Jinx, Making a Murderer — the investigative documentary tradition

script.tone:
The writing tone must be that of an experienced investigative journalist who has learned to let the facts speak louder than the voice. The narration never shouts. It never speculates beyond what the evidence supports. It never tells the viewer what to conclude — it lays out the contradiction and allows the weight of it to do the work.

Every sentence must be earned by evidence. If a claim cannot be sourced, it does not appear in the script. If something is genuinely unknown, the script says so explicitly — 'this has never been explained publicly' is more powerful than any invented conclusion.

The tone should move between:

Cold and factual — when presenting documented evidence
Controlled and tense — when the contradiction in the official story is revealed
Deliberately restrained — when others would sensationalise, pull back
Precise and measured — when handling the most serious claims
Minimally philosophical — at the end, when the implication is left to sit

Key writing characteristics:

Open with a documented contradiction — not a theory, a fact that conflicts with the official account
Use primary sources wherever possible: court transcripts, government documents, whistleblower testimony, declassified files
Never use passive conspiracy framing ('some say', 'many believe') without immediately grounding it in specific evidence
Build the investigation like a legal case: establish the facts, identify the gaps, present the witness testimony, reveal the structural motive
Every claim should be at minimum inferrable from documented reality
Use the word 'allegedly' and 'according to [specific source]' with precision — they mean something in this format
Never resolve the investigation conclusively — the strongest ending is one that leaves the viewer with the right question, not the answer
Treat every subject — regardless of power or notoriety — with the same journalistic distance
The viewer should feel at the end of every video that they now understand why this matters, even if they do not know the full truth
script.hook: Open with a specific documented contradiction — a date, a name, a statement, and the evidence that directly conflicts with it. No framing. No context. Just the contradiction, stated flatly, as if reading from a document. The power is in the gap between what was said and what the record shows.
script.celebrities: Edward Snowden, Julian Assange, Daniel Ellsberg, Jeffrey Epstein, Ghislaine Maxwell, William Colby, Frank Church, Gary Webb, Bob Woodward, Karen Silkwood, Enron executives, Cambridge Analytica, Mark Zuckerberg in congressional testimony, Harvey Weinstein, John DeLorean, Sam Bankman-Fried, Rupert Murdoch, Boris Johnson, Nixon, Kissinger, Allen Dulles, J. Edgar Hoover, COINTELPRO subjects, MK-Ultra subjects (documented), Church Committee testimonies
script.lesson: Never a lesson in the traditional sense. The video ends with the most significant unresolved question the evidence raises. It is not a conclusion — it is a door left open. The viewer should close the video feeling that they now see something clearly that they cannot unsee, and that they must decide for themselves what to do with it.
script.forbidden_words: they, the elites, wake up, sheeple, the truth is out there, nobody wants you to know, they don't want you to see this, deep state (without documented sourcing), red pill, hoax, crisis actor
script.power_words: documented, contradicted, redacted, declassified, testimony, evidence, gap, motive, suppressed, unreported, corroborated, record, discrepancy, classified, oversight

visuals.vis_palette: Black, white, government red, dim yellow, cold fluorescent grey
visuals.vis_accent_color: Government red for classifications and warnings, cold white for evidence labels, dim yellow for timestamps and dates
visuals.vis_broll_rules:
hidden/classified = document folder being opened slowly, manila envelope on a desk, file cabinet drawer sliding out
control/surveillance = CCTV camera on a building corner, data centre hallway, satellite dish array, server room
money_trail = wire transfer documents, offshore account paper trail, numbered accounts, money moving through financial diagrams
dead/redacted = official document with thick black redaction bars, censored photograph, file stamped CLASSIFIED
truth/revelation = static on a television suddenly clearing, a single light turned on in a dark room, newspaper front page zoomed in
power = empty senate chambers, UN assembly from the back, presidential motorcade from a distance, security perimeter
evidence = physical file laid out on table under single lamp, photograph printed and pinned to a board, handwritten notes in a ledger
covered_up = shredder running at night in an empty office, tape recorder being switched off, documents burning
whistleblower = person in silhouette in a car park, pay phone in the rain, anonymous source walking away from camera
government = brutalist government architecture from below, empty corridor of a federal building, flag in rain
courtroom = courthouse steps, legal documents, hands sorting through evidence files
press = camera bulbs flashing at a press conference, headlines printing, news anchor speaking on muted monitor
deception = two faces side by side where one is partially obscured, shadow cast by a figure off screen, handshake with one hand hidden
motive = organizational chart with lines of influence, financial records, timeline board
gap = black space where a document should be, blank section of a report, 'NO RECORD FOUND' text
testimony = close-up of hands folded on a witness table, microphone in front of empty chair, deposition room
propaganda = old broadcast footage with the sound removed, news ticker scrolling at speed, identical headlines from different outlets side by side
visuals.vis_aroll_rule: Narrator voice drives the investigation — the narrator should rarely if ever appear on camera. When real people appear, they should be archival footage, documentary clips, congressional testimony, press conference recordings, or court appearances. Never staged recreation. Never re-enactment. Only the documented record. Archival clips should play for no more than 8 seconds before narrator context resumes.
visuals.vis_forbidden: Paranoid visual clichés: red string on corkboards used unironically, tinfoil hat imagery, alien imagery unless directly relevant, shadowy figures in hooded cloaks, lightning storm footage used as generic atmosphere. Any footage that appears staged or recreated without clear disclosure. Anonymous social media posts presented as evidence. Meme-format graphics. Talking heads without verified identification. Facial recognition overlays on private individuals. Sensationalist news lower-thirds borrowed from tabloid sources. Upbeat or triumphant music. Any visual that looks like it came from a flat-earther video. Unnamed sources presented as credible without documented corroboration.

mograph.triggers:
* hidden = slow-motion cascade of classified document pages falling across a black background, each stamped with a different government classification seal
* control = overhead view of a surveillance network map — glowing nodes appearing one by one connected by cold blue lines across a dark globe
* money_trail = animated line tracing through a series of offshore account labels across a dark map, each jump labeled with a dollar amount
* dead = single candle extinguishing in darkness, smoke curling upward before a redacted document fades in over the blank frame
* truth = television static slowly resolving into a single still image — cold, underlit, ambiguous
* power = chess pieces arranged on a board in darkness, one moves — the others react in slow motion cascade
* evidence = manila folder dropping onto a table in extreme slow motion, papers fanning out across a surface under a single lamp
* covered = black rectangular bars appearing progressively over a document until only fragments of text remain visible
* whistleblower = single pay phone in rain under a streetlight, receiver hanging — camera slowly pushes in
* gap = document being viewed where a crucial paragraph slowly becomes blank, text dissolving to empty white space
* motive = organizational chart building itself — names appearing, lines connecting, until a pattern becomes unmistakably visible
* testimony = empty chair at a witness table with a microphone, single overhead light, long table receding into shadow
* suppressed = radio signal waveform appearing then being jammed — flatlines into silence with a single sustained tone
* redacted = words appearing on screen one by one then being crossed out in red before the next word appears
* discrepancy = two identical documents side by side with one key line highlighted in each — the lines contradict each other
* classified = government stamp descending slowly over a document in real time, ink spreading from the stamp's contact point
mograph.style:
The motion graphics language of CONSPIRACY should feel like a classified document aesthetic crossed with an investigative documentary evidence board. Cold, precise, and institutional — never sensationalist.

Core aesthetic rules:

* Black and deep grey backgrounds only — no colour gradients, no soft atmospheres
* Red is used exclusively for classification warnings, contradiction markers, and critical evidence labels — use it sparingly so it retains weight
* White is for facts, testimony, documents — the clean language of evidence
* All text should appear as if typed or stamped rather than designed — monospace fonts for documents and dates, bold condensed for headlines
* Document overlays should include subtle paper texture and slight yellowing to suggest age where appropriate
* All data visualizations (timelines, org charts, money trails) should look like they were produced by an investigative team, not a motion designer — functional over beautiful
* Use subtle scanlines and CRT distortion on archival footage to visually code it as 'past evidence'
* Use a crisp, clean frame for narrator-presented evidence — the contrast between the messy archival world and the clean presentation should be visible
* Timelines should be simple horizontal or vertical lines with date stamps — no animations beyond a slow camera move along the timeline
* Organizational charts should animate one connection at a time, with each connection triggering as the narrator names the link
* Redaction bars should animate — appearing letter by letter or sliding across text — to make the act of suppression feel visible and active
* All lower thirds should use a government document aesthetic: thin red line, white text, monospace date/location, classification-style label
* Nothing should look designed for aesthetics — everything should look designed for investigation
mograph.text_style: Monospace font for all evidence labels, dates, and document fragments. Bold condensed sans-serif for investigation headlines. All caps for classification stamps and section headers. White on black for facts. Red on black for contradictions or gaps. Text appears as if being typed in real time for document sequences. Maximum 6 words for overlay statements. Evidence labels remain on screen for 4 seconds minimum.

sound.mood:
The sound design should feel like the score to a serious documentary about institutional power — cold, atmospheric, deeply unsettling without ever becoming horror-movie clichéd. The reference points are Disasterpeace's more ambient work, Jonny Greenwood's film scores, and the eerie restraint of an Errol Morris documentary score.

The music should feel like the audio equivalent of reading a document you were never supposed to see.

For the opening:

* Begin with near-silence — one low sustained drone, barely audible
* A single distant piano note or distant string harmonic
* The silence between sounds is more important than the sounds themselves
* Let the investigation set up before the music begins to intrude
* The opening should feel like the moments before something classified is revealed

For investigation and evidence sections:

* Low, sustained drones — cold, hovering
* Occasional sparse piano — single notes with long reverb tails
* Subtle metallic textures — the sound of institutional machinery
* Faint rhythmic pulse (not a beat — more like a heartbeat or a distant clock)
* Music should create tension without announcing itself

For revelation moments (when evidence contradicts official story):

* Music drops to near-silence
* Single low bass note sustains
* Then silence again — let the evidence breathe
* Return slowly with a slightly more dissonant version of the main theme

For the final unresolved question:

* Music becomes sparser
* Sustained string tone that does not resolve
* Ends on an open chord — never a conclusion in the music, because there is no conclusion in the investigation

Avoid:

* Dramatic 'conspiracy theory' sound design — the deep male voice, the dramatic sting, the thunder sound effect
* Horror movie jump-scare audio design
* Triumphant or resolved musical endings
* Anything that sounds like a YouTube documentary about aliens
* Music that makes the viewer feel told what to feel rather than unsettled by what they are thinking

The goal: the score should make the viewer feel the weight of institutional reality — that real power is quiet, patient, and very comfortable with silence.
sound.ambient: Distant office hum, faint reel-to-reel tape running, old HVAC in an empty government building, the ambience of a room that has been listening for a long time
sound.silence: Music drops to complete silence when the central documented contradiction of the investigation is revealed. Narrator speaks the contradiction into total silence. Silence holds for 3–5 seconds after the statement. Then a single low tone re-enters. This silence should feel like the moment a jury hears evidence it cannot ignore.
sound.swells: First swell at the point where the gap in the official story becomes undeniable — the evidence that cannot be explained away. Music rises slightly, then retreats. Second swell when the structural motive is established — why this could have happened this way. Final moment: not a swell but a withdrawal — music fades to almost nothing as the final unresolved question is delivered.
sound.extra: All archival audio — press conferences, testimony recordings, intercepted communications — should be treated with a subtle low-pass filter and slight room reverb to make them sound like they are being played from a physical tape or broadcast. This codes them instantly as 'the record' rather than 'content'. Narrator voice should be clean and unprocessed — the sharpest, clearest voice in the mix. When transitioning between time periods, use a brief audio cut to a different ambient tone (a different room, a different era's room noise) before the music re-enters.

editing.edit_pacing:
Opening contradiction = slow and deliberate. Do not rush. The contradiction should be stated at measured pace — let each piece of information land before the next arrives. Cut every 6–10 seconds. The viewer needs to be oriented before the pace increases.

Evidence building sections = patient and methodical. Cut every 7–12 seconds. Let archival footage and documents breathe. Use slow camera moves over documents rather than rapid cuts. The pace of evidence presentation should mirror the pace of a real investigation — careful, not exciting.

Contradiction reveal sequences = slightly faster, 3–6 seconds per cut. The contrast between official claim and documented evidence should be presented in rapid alternation — claim, evidence, claim, evidence — until the gap is undeniable. Then stop. Hold the final frame.

Structural analysis (motive, power, connections) = medium pacing, 5–8 seconds. Use this time to build the organizational picture — slow pans over charts, deliberate reveals of connections, names appearing one at a time.

Final unresolved question = very slow. One shot per idea. 10–15 seconds per cut. The viewer should feel the weight of what they now understand. Do not rush them toward the end — let them arrive there at the pace of genuine realisation.

General rules:

* Never use a fast cut for its own sake — every cut must introduce new information or a new perspective
* Use hard cuts between claims and the evidence that contradicts them — the juxtaposition is the argument
* Use slow fades only at major structural transitions (time period changes, subject shifts)
* Let archival audio play under a relevant visual rather than always cutting to the source — sometimes the sound of a document being described is more powerful than the image of it
* Use freeze frames on faces at moments of documented deception — hold for 2–3 extra seconds after the narrator names what they said
editing.edit_text_rules: All on-screen text should look like documentary evidence markers — not YouTube graphics. Dates appear in monospace at the bottom left, formatted as DAY MONTH YEAR. Source attribution appears as a small white label at the bottom right when archival footage is playing. Key contradictions are highlighted by placing the conflicting claims side by side in split text panels with a thin red line between them. Document fragments that appear on screen should be legible at standard viewing distance — no decorative illegibility. Maximum 6 words for narrator-support overlays. Evidence labels stay on screen until the narrator has moved past them.
editing.edit_end: Final 20 seconds: the investigation does not conclude. The narrator delivers the final unresolved question over a single wide, cold shot — an empty government building, a locked door, a document still marked classified. Music withdraws to silence. The final line is delivered into near-silence. 4 seconds of held silence on the final image before fade to black. No subscribe animation. No channel logo appearance during these final seconds. Let the ending exist undisturbed.
editing.edit_special:
Every episode should include one dedicated 'evidence presentation' sequence — structured like a journalist laying out documents for the first time. Elements animate one at a time: photograph pinned to board, date written next to it, connecting line drawn to the next piece. The pace is deliberately slow. The viewer should feel like they are inside the investigation, not watching a finished product.

When presenting conflicting testimony from two sources, use a split-screen or rapid alternation structure — Source A states X, Source B states the opposite, the contradiction held visually for 3 seconds before the narrator notes the discrepancy.

For any subject who later recanted or modified their official statement, a text lower third should appear at the moment of their original statement showing what they said publicly — and a second lower third showing what they later admitted or denied. The gap between the two should be visible and held on screen for at least 3 seconds.

narrator.persona: A veteran investigative journalist who has spent two decades watching official stories collapse under the weight of evidence they produced themselves. Speaks with controlled, earned authority — never angry, never sensational, never afraid of silence. The voice of someone who has learned that the most powerful thing you can do is state the documented record clearly and step back. There is no performance in this voice — only the weight of what has been found.
narrator.role: The narrator is the spine of every investigation. They establish the contradiction, present the evidence, name the gaps, provide the structural context, and deliver the final unresolved question. They introduce every piece of archival testimony and recontextualise it for the viewer. They are not a commentator — they are an investigator presenting findings. The investigation belongs to the narrator; the evidence is what makes it credible.
narrator.doc_structure: Narrator opens by stating a specific documented contradiction — flat, factual, no framing. Camera cuts to relevant archival footage or document. Narrator begins to establish context: who, when, where, and what the official account says. First testimony or evidence clip appears — an interview, a document, a recording. Narrator re-enters to identify what that evidence contradicts in the official account. Second evidence layer deepens the gap. Narrator establishes the structural motive — why this could have happened this way, who benefited, what the documented incentives were. Third evidence layer — the most significant, held longest. Narrator delivers the implication without concluding. Final question posed. Music withdraws. Silence. Fade.

shorts.formula:
Every Short is built around one documented fact that directly contradicts something the viewer was told — or assumed — was true. No theories. No speculation. One verifiable contradiction, presented flatly.

▸ SECONDS 0–3: The contradiction stated as a plain fact. Large text on screen immediately. No introduction.
▸ SECONDS 3–12: Who said what, when, and what the record actually shows. One piece of evidence.
▸ SECONDS 12–40: The gap — why this matters, what it means, why it has never been adequately explained.
▸ SECONDS 40–55: The implication delivered without a conclusion. One question, stated once.
▸ SECONDS 55–60: Silence. The question on screen. Fade to black.

The Short ends when the contradiction has been clearly established — not when it has been resolved. It never resolves.
shorts.hook: First frame: the contradiction in plain language. A date, a name, a claim, and the evidence that disproves it — stated immediately and without warmup. If it is an archival clip, it begins mid-sentence at the most revealing moment. Large white text on screen from frame one. No logo. No channel name. No context. The contradiction is the entire hook.
shorts.visuals: Cold, controlled, and documentary in feel. Black and white or deeply desaturated for archival sections. Document overlays are always legible. No sensationalist visuals. No red-string corkboards. Text is in monospace for evidence labels and bold condensed for statements. Face — if present — is archival footage, not the narrator. Cuts are slow by Shorts standards — 4–6 seconds per visual during evidence presentation, faster (2–3 seconds) during the contradiction reveal. The visual language should say: this is a serious investigation, not entertainment.
shorts.music: Near-silence or a single sustained low tone throughout. No trending audio. Music, if present at all, is a barely audible atmospheric layer — never melodic, never rhythmic. During the central contradiction, music disappears entirely. Returns as a single sustained note for the final question. The Short should feel uncomfortably quiet.

thumbnail.thumb_concept:
Primary concept: a visual representation of the gap between the official account and what the evidence shows. This means: a redacted document with one visible line, a silhouetted figure in an official setting, a split image where one side is an official photograph and the other is the contradicting evidence, or a single face — archival, not posed — at a moment of documented deception.

Colour palette:
black + cold white = official record, institutional silence
black + government red = contradiction, suppression, classified
dim yellow + black = archival, historical, buried
black + white = documentary authority, undeniable record

Style: the thumbnail should look like it belongs in an investigative journalism context — not on a conspiracy channel. Editorial, restrained, serious. If a viewer saw it without the channel name, they should think: this looks like a serious documentary investigation.
thumbnail.thumb_font: Bebas Neue for investigation headlines, monospace for evidence labels and dates — all caps, white on dark, no shadow effects that obscure legibility
thumbnail.thumb_text_formula: Create a specific knowledge gap using a documented fact or a named contradiction. The viewer should feel that they are missing a specific piece of information — not a vague conspiracy, but a verifiable gap. Examples: WHAT THE FILE SAYS / THE TESTIMONY THEY BURIED / HE CONTRADICTED HIMSELF / THE DATE THAT DOESN'T ADD UP / WHAT WAS REDACTED. Never use exclamation marks. Never imply certainty beyond what the evidence supports.
thumbnail.thumb_title_formula:
The [document/testimony/date] that [reveals/contradicts/changes] everything
What [agency/person/institution] said — and what the record shows
[Name]: the [gap/contradiction/evidence] nobody explained
The [classified/redacted/suppressed] [document/report/testimony] of [year/event]
Why [official account of event] still doesn't add up
The [number] things [investigation] never answered
What [witness/document/recording] actually said about [event]

All titles under 70 characters. Use the specific language of investigation: 'the record', 'the testimony', 'the document'. Never use 'secret' or 'they don't want you to know'. The title should feel like a headline from a serious investigative outlet.
thumbnail.thumb_forbidden: Red string corkboards. Shadowed mysterious figures in cloaks. Tinfoil hat imagery. Lightning and thunder backgrounds. Question marks in neon. Alien imagery unless directly relevant and evidenced. Social media post screenshots. Anonymous source quotes. Any face that appears digitally manipulated. Sensationalist red-and-yellow colour combinations. Any text that uses exclamation marks. Multiple conflicting visual elements that create clutter. Anything that a viewer would immediately associate with fringe YouTube content.

seo.description:
SEO Package — CONSPIRACY channel

Titles should combine investigative language with the specific event, person, or institution:

Searchable terms to include naturally:
documentary, investigation, whistleblower, declassified, government, cover-up (used in journalistic context), evidence, testimony, true story, explained, history, what really happened

Description template:
In [year/period], [subject] made a public statement about [topic]. What the record actually shows is something different. In this investigation, we examine [specific contradiction] using [document type / testimony type / archival source].

This is not a theory. These are documented facts.

🎬 More investigations: [link]

00:00 – The contradiction
[timestamps for each section]

#documentary #investigation #{topic} #{event or person} #evidence

Description should never use the word 'conspiracy' in the first two lines. Lead with the journalistic framing. Use the word 'documented' where possible. The description should establish credibility before it establishes intrigue.
seo.tags: documentary, investigation, whistleblower, declassified documents, government cover-up, true story, investigative journalism, evidence, testimony, history exposed, what really happened
seo.cta: If you believe in evidence over assumption, subscribe. We investigate one story at a time — using primary sources, documented testimony, and the kind of rigour that used to exist in journalism. Leave a comment with the story you think deserves investigation next.
